attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man is admitted to the palliative care ward for end-of-life care for metastatic colorectal cancer. He has a continuous subcutaneous infusion containing morphine, midazolam, and glycopyrrolate. He does not take any other regular medications. His medical history includes a right hemicolectomy two years ago.

On examination, he appears comfortable at rest but requires occasional breakthrough subcutaneous morphine for pain, which appears to be effective. He is becoming increasingly drowsy but rousable. His family is becoming distressed by his episodes of breakthrough pain and are requesting increased medication doses.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Increase all medications in the continuous subcutaneous infusion
Increase the morphine dose in the continuous subcutaneous infusion
Discuss expectations and care of normal dying process
Commence additional regular breakthrough doses of morphine
Counsel that patient is comfortable with current pain regimen
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a common challenge in end-of-life care where there needs to be a balance between adequate symptom control and maintaining an appropriate level of consciousness, while supporting the family through this difficult time.

Discuss expectations and care of normal dying process is the most appropriate next step. This allows exploration of their concerns and expectations, explanation of the normal dying process, and education about the balance between pain control and alertness. The family's distress needs to be acknowledged and addressed, while providing reassurance that breakthrough pain is being appropriately managed with the current regimen.

Increasing all medications in the continuous subcutaneous infusion would be inappropriate as the current breakthrough pain management appears effective. The patient is already becoming drowsy, and increasing all medications could unnecessarily compromise consciousness and interaction with family during valuable remaining time.

Increasing the morphine dose in the continuous subcutaneous infusion alone would not be indicated as the current pain management strategy with breakthrough doses appears effective. Increasing the background dose may lead to increased drowsiness without additional benefit.

Commencing additional regular breakthrough doses of morphine would risk over-sedation and respiratory depression. Breakthrough doses should remain available as needed rather than scheduled regularly, as the current approach appears to be working.

Counselling that the patient is comfortable without addressing the family's concerns would be dismissive of their distress and miss an opportunity for important end-of-life care discussions. While the current pain management may be clinically appropriate, the family's emotional needs must also be addressed.

The key principles in this situation are maintaining open communication with the family, providing education about the dying process, and finding the right balance between comfort and consciousness according to the patient and family's wishes. Regular reassessment of both pain control and sedation levels should guide any medication adjustments.
learningPoints: End-of-life care requires balancing symptom control with maintaining consciousness as per patient and family wishes.
Clear communication about the goals of care and expected progression is essential in palliative care.
Regular assessment and documentation of both pain and sedation levels helps guide appropriate medication titration.
Family and caregivers of patients undergoing palliative care are at high risk of psychological distress and open communication and support should be offered
references: Powazki RD, Walsh D, Aktas A, Hauser K. Palliative Medicine Family Conferences Reduce Spokesperson Distress and Enhance Communication in Advanced Cancer. J Palliat Med. 2018;21(8):1086-1093. doi:10.1089/jpm.2018.0143. Available online at ; https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29727264/

Oechsle K, Ullrich A, Marx G, Benze G, Heine J, Dickel LM, Zhang Y, Wowretzko F, Wendt KN, Nauck F, Bokemeyer C. Psychological burden in family caregivers of patients with advanced cancer at initiation of specialist inpatient palliative care. BMC Palliative Care. 2019 Dec;18:1-4. Available online at ; https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1186/s12904-019-0469-7.pdf
